transcript.text: Hey, sorry.
Happy Monday.
How are you?
Happy Monday.
I'm good.
Thanks.
How are you?
Pretty good.
I did a bunch of like organizing things, putting things away in the garage.
Like I'm super sore today, but you know.
Yeah.
Pretty good.
How was your weekend?
It was good.
Yeah.
Pretty low key.
Last weekend I was traveling for a wedding.
So this weekend it was nice to just relax a little bit.
Oh my God.
Is it already wedding season?
Yeah.
I don't think it is.
It was just like a really random one-off wedding.
I know.
It's like usually January is not a big wedding season or time, but that's good.
Are you in the city?
I think you're in the Bay Area, right?
Are you in the city or where are you based?
Yeah.
I live right by Alamo Square Park.
Oh, nice.
Nice.
I'm in Oakland.
So then days I don't have a lot of in-person things.
mostly work from home.
And then the, they usually go in like Tuesday and Thursdays and then.
And if there's, I try to cluster things a lot, you know, but yeah, how's her great things from Bridget?
How's the first conversation?
You talked to her.
Have you talked to anyone else yet?
Just Tucker and her.
Yeah.
Okay, perfect.
Yeah.
Like first impressions, thoughts from the first conversations?
Yeah, I really enjoyed it.
Honestly.
Yeah, I'm super excited about the opportunity.
It seems really interesting.
seems like a really cool inflection point for the company and like a lot of momentum.
And so it seems like a lot of work to be done, but really interesting work.
Yeah.
Yeah.

And maybe it would be a good place to start, even though like I read notes and, you know, look to your profile, but always good to hear in your own voice a little bit of the super TLDR journey plus like kind of what you're thinking about if you were to take a new step, you know, or a new chapter for your journey.
For sure.
Yeah.
So started off in consulting, spent a couple of years there, but really felt that itch for.
For For sure.
More of an impact and really to be more of an operator and like do more execution.
So that's what led me to the startup world where it was at Mixpanel.
So at Mixpanel, I did really kind of helping with setting operational cadence and operational structure.
So setting company KPIs and then working with each function to set their KPIs and their strategic initiatives.
And then Airtable was really focused more on special projects.
So the main special project in the time I was there was executing on a new pricing and packaging model that we had just kind of created.
And so working really closely with go-to-market teams to make sure we're executing well and the pricing is achieving what we want it to.
And then both those places were a little bit on the bigger side.
So I wanted to try something smaller.
So that's what took me to Everlance.
At Everlance, was about 30 people when I started.
And I had kind of a unique role where I started off as an IC, but the situation we were in was we basically had sold a product that we hadn't built out yet.
And so as starting...
And so there are kind of two goals.
One was work with our product team to actually build out the platform in a very self-serve and very scalable way.
And two was in the meantime, work with the functions that are supporting the product to make their processes as efficient and as effective as possible.
And then kind of how my journey went is as I took on each process, I ended up taking on the teams under them as well.
So started with implementation, then reimbursement operations and customer success and customer support.
So that's kind of where I sit today over the entire post-sales.
And about nine months ago, we got acquired, which was really exciting.
But the company that acquired us is just very slow-moving, very legacy, you know.
Yeah, exactly.
So, yeah, that's just led me to see what else might be out there now that we're kind of wrapping up the integration work.
Nice, nice.
And before I kind of jump in, give you like a high level, what drives you right now?
Like what's important to you?
Like it's.
As far as like both what energizes you, but also what's motivating and driving you from a professional side?
Yeah, I think the biggest thing that drives me is impact.
So getting to see the impact of my work is really what gives me a sense of purpose in what I'm doing every day.

And so being in a role where I can really execute on things, really help drive the direction of the company, work really closely cross-functionally, and kind of get to be kind of that Swiss knife operator that really gets to touch all parts of the business.
I really love that.
And yeah, I'm just looking to help another company scale and grow.
And that's what I love to do.
I love to get that momentum and capture and, you know, keep writing in.
And so, yeah, I think that's really what I'm looking for.
And like the other thing is just like making sure I'm working with people who I enjoy working with and who are excited about what we're building together.
I think that was my favorite part of everything.
And so I think that's what I'm looking to recreate again.
Yeah, that's awesome.
And I'm assuming Tucker and Bridget gave you some.
But would it be helpful to give like a high level as well?
Yeah, that would be great.
Yeah.
Just in your own words.
Yeah.
Yeah.
So the I think our company has had a unique journey in that we started with really kind of like a service model.
Right.
And part of that was ultimately like I've been a CMO, had to grow with marketing for the last 15 years.
And in my last company, was starting to build a lot of AI workflows to help us grow organically through content.
And we ended up 24x in our traffic.
The company I was working at, Deepgram, recently raised a Series C.
One of their board members is our main investor who's on our board.
Right.
Like, so there's a lot of signals of like what they saw, like the work do.
And so two years, almost two years ago, April of 2024, I started doing workshops of just teaching people how to build AI workflows and around.
so lot of
You know, content and helping companies grow organically, right?
And the first one we had 33 people pay 500 bucks for, and three of those became customers.
And from there, we did another workshop and another workshop, and I was still like CMO of DeepGram.
And so by September of that year, it was really clear we were over a million and a half in revenue.
We had a lot of customers like Ramp and others that, you know, there's something here.
So that's when I quit my job.
And at the beginning, we really wanted to really think of it as like, hey, we're a marketing agency that's AI powered, and we're for the ploy kind of model, if you will.
And as a CMO, I just wanted to hire one partner to just figure it out, right?
Like I didn't want to have to stitch together like a platform, tools, agencies, freelancers, internal hires, all of that.
And so that was kind of like the model that was true.
Completely kind of like a no-brainer, right?
Like you pay $9, today's $18 a month, and you get a strategist, you get an editor in the loop, if you will, and we build the workflows internally.
We use our workflows, and we create content for you and optimize it and do kind of A to Z.

And things just kept scaling, and, you know, last year we went from close to $2 to $12 million in ARR, and this year our goal is to go from $12 to $36, and, you know, we're a little over 90 people now serving a little over 60 customers.
Our average customers paying us over $200,000 a year, but because of our model, right, we, there's a lot of, it's an operationally heavy business.
We're still close to 70% gross margin, so still softer margin, but out of like 90 or so people, almost 55 of those are in the delivery team, and there's kind of like almost...
There's two sides of the business.
There's the build and there's the deliverer side of the business.
So that adds a decent amount of complexity.
And then as our platform and our systems evolve, the kinds of roles we need on the delivery side also evolve.
And then as the business expands, how those roles should be day-to-day, renewals is more of a focus than it was last year.
We're going to have a lot to renew.
Right.
And, um, and so things start to like, you start to standardize and then, you know, then scale.
And, and then I think like we've kept the organization fairly flat, but now we're at a place where there is a lot of like growing pains on like, Hey, we're a 12 plus million dollar business now.
And we need to act accordingly.
We can't just like, you know, ask Marcel everything.
And, um, and so kind of, that's where, where we are.
And, um, we're bringing in like, uh, a head of like customer, if you will, um, that she's been working.

And we're hiring a lot in that role, but we need a lot of support on kind of like an extension of me to really kind of run the operations, if you will, but also be nimble enough and flexible enough to be forward-deploying special projects as needed as well, you know, to kind of be an extension of me in things that maybe I can't be involved every single day on, you know.
Yeah, totally.
That sounds super interesting.
And yeah, that's kind of how Bridget and Tucker described it.
Seems like a really interesting inflection point and actually quite similar to my time at Everlance because like I mentioned, we hadn't built out the product yet.
So, and it's a tool that reimburses people tax-free for their mileage that they've driven for work.

And so really we were basically like professional services because our customer success agents were like doing everything in the backend and just telling admin the information they couldn't see anything other than the miles driven.
And so we built that from that to a platform and then had to kind of evolve our customer success organization along that.
So,
It was a really interesting journey.
so, yeah, sounds really exciting to get a chance to do it again.
Yeah, that's really cool.
Love to dig into that.
So what was the impetus or the trigger, if you will, when you were at Airtable to think of leaving or wanting to leave?
And then tell me more about from there, what led you to Everlance?
Yeah.
So at Airtable, think the main impetus was my manager left to go to business school.
And then I was kind of moved under the finance team.
And then the work was just not what I was used to doing.
It wasn't like the exciting special projects.
was a lot of kind of just like moving things around.
Like it wasn't really high impact work.

And then because it was bureaucratic, like when I would bring up these projects and ideas of things I wanted to work on, it was just kind of hard to get them approved or like get them, like get the permission to work on them, which like at Everlance, like that's like you just...
Just do things.
If you see something that was wrong, you just go do it, right?
Like you get like seven layers of approval.
So that was kind of the main impetus to leave.
And then, yeah, when I got to Everlands, kind of the first big project I took on was revamping.
And what was the role at Everlands specifically when you first joined?
It was just a biz ops and strategy general IC role.
Yeah.
Tell me more like for that role, like what was kind of like the mandate, if you will, right?

Almost like a summary of the job description of like the first 30 days of like you join is like, you know, there's always a job description, but then there's like when you first come is like, this is what we actually need you for, you know?
Yeah, that mandate was really those two pieces I mentioned before.
One was help build out the product to be really self-serve and really scalable since it's not built out.
And two, like just find whatever's on fire.
Like all of our processes, we have a ton of different processes going.
It was very operationally heavy.
We had a lot of people on the operations team figure out what How big was the company when you joined?
It was about 35 people.
Okay, cool.
Nice.
So our operation.
The team was about 10 people then.
And then who were you reporting to when you first joined?
I was reporting to the COO.
Okay, nice.
And what was the COO's first name, just so I can reference the person?
Yeah, yeah, Dan.
Dan, okay, cool.
Okay, so Dan was essentially the person to hire you?
Yeah, he was, Okay, cool.
And then, okay, so you come in and it's like, hey, we don't have a product, we need to figure that out.
And hey, we need to kind of evolve things operationally.
That sounds like the two, too many things, right?
What was a good, like, first year project that you worked on that felt, like, kind of meaty?
And it can be a project or it can be, like, a phase of a thing that was ongoing, you know?
Yeah.
Yeah, I think the biggest thing that I worked on the first year was our reimbursement operations.
So that was really, like, the core of how we reimburse drivers.
And so, like I mentioned, none of it was really built in the product.
So it was all run kind of in spreadsheets, index match.
Like, I'm sure you can imagine kind of a disaster.
Yeah.
With these people kind of like executing on it, on the operations team.

And so what was really complex about this was I had to work with product to build out features while working with the operations team to improve their process in parallel and also making sure they remained in lockstep because whenever product would launch something, all the automations, all the processes had to change on the operations side, right?
And so it was really like maintaining coordination and then making sure product launches what they say they would launch and that it works the way that it should.
And then also making sure we like adapt on the operations team.
Okay.
So just to play it back.
So at some point they said, Hey, today, if you said a driver submits expenses, right?
There's like some process currently in place.
And then the directive, if I'm understanding correctly, was like, help us make this a little bit more scalable before it becomes part of them.
product.
Is that a good way to play back?
Yeah, definitely.
And I think it was make it more scalable, but also like make it more accurate.
Our accuracy rate was very poor.
One out of 10 payments were being made incorrectly.
So we had a 10% error rate and we had a 20% delay rate.
So 20% of our customers were getting paid late.

So it was kind of, that's one of the reasons why we had to do this kind of parallel processing of improving the product and improving the process at the same time, because it wasn't like we could just build out the product and then migrate everybody because the process itself was failing so badly, you know?
Got it.
Okay.
So, so if I understand, like, so they were selling to companies or, or, or whatever, right?
And, and then the specific users within those companies were drivers.
Yes.
And then the drivers would log in to the platform to submit expenses?
Well, they would have, they had an app on their phone that would track the mileage that they drove.
And then they submit their report at the end of the month with the number of miles they drove.
Do their manner.
In the app or something?
to their manager.
And then what we would do is we would calculate the tax-free amount that they would be reimbursed based on their vehicle type, their location.
There's kind of a model in the back in a spreadsheet at the beginning that would calculate kind of what their exact monthly rate should be for variable and for their fixed rate.
And then we would kind of calculate how much their total payment should be, pay it, and then also figure out their tax liability on the back end.
If they aren't compliant.
And there, you mean the companies or the individual user?
The individual's tax liability.
Got it.
Okay.
And this was, and I wasn't as familiar with the platform, but this is a platform for individual users or for businesses?
So we actually had two pieces of our business.
We had the B2C business, which was kind of a cash cow.
And that was so individual users, like DoorDash drivers, gig workers could use it to get tax breaks.
And then on the B2B side, we would sell the platform to the companies.
And there.
Administrators would use kind of the dashboard, the drivers would just use the app.
And so initially all the mileage driven in the app.
Okay.
So that part, the project you worked on, was it more focused on the individual?
No.
So it was focused on the reimbursement process on the B2B side.
So basically everything from administrator, it was actually less focused on the individual drivers, more on the administrator's experience.
Okay, cool.
So I think I understand.
So there's like business admins, like company admins, and they're the ones kind of like managing the whole thing.
The individual drivers, you know, drive and track a mileage and then submit some kind of expense or something.
And then there is like magic voodoo process over here that you had to improve that then.
And then when that process is done, it sends it back to both, you know, like the company admin and the driver, I'm assuming.
So it sends it to the admin.
And then we would issue the payments for them also.
So we had a third party, now it's built into the product, but before we had a third party that we would use to send the payments.
So that was also another step of the process was once we get approval, sending the payments to drivers, verifying receipt, all that.
Okay, perfect.
That makes sense.
And so what was the first thing you did, the project, like, hey, we need help with this.
Walk me through a little bit of the milestones and kind of how you approached the project.
Yeah.
So I think the first thing was really taking stock of what the process looks like today.

So just sitting through one of the processes, seeing where the key sources of error come from and where we're, what are the situations that really, like, cause issues and then looking on the product side.
And so they were just starting to like build out any semblance of a platform.
And so what existed today versus what needed to exist and then figuring out kind of what is the first step that we need product to build that would make our lives on the operation side easier.
So just baselining first, and then from there, figuring out what the first next step should be.
So one easy kind of low-hanging fruit that was there was like, we didn't have the rates that customers should get paid in the platform.
And so because of that, we would export the mileage and then index match the rate, which has a very high error rate, lots of issues.
If a name is spelled slightly differently, it's really hard, right?
And so the first thing was really just, let's have a system where we can upload a CSV of all the rates into the dashboard.
So that at least at each driver's profile, now we are going to be able to export their mileage and their rate.
And then the next step after that can be, we can multiply them.
And then we can go, you know, just like, let's build this super, super incrementally.
And then that way, we on the A, like the changes are easy to execute on the product side.
So we're at less risk of errors or things being built incorrectly.
But then B, like our operations team can really easily anticipate the changes and how they'll affect their workflow.
And then that we can kind of do that in a way that tackles each major pain point one at a time so that their jobs just get progressively easier versus like launching five things at once.
And then now operations team is kind of scrambling to figure out what has changed and what is launched.
Got it.
That makes sense.
And like looking back at that project, right?
Like at what point did you like, was there like kind of like a milestone or kind of a handoff, you know, and kind of like, yeah, tell me more about that.
Yeah, I think the milestone and handoff ultimately happened once we got to a place where most of it was built out into the product.
So basically what we built out was a fixed pay period and a variable pay period, which had all of their reports with their mileage and how much they should get paid.
So that was all there.
And then.
And we had also built out a kind of a invoice that would provide the information for that month from both the fixed and the variable pay period, how much each person should get paid.
And that was all in the product.
We still actually use the operations team for QA just because we this is such a highly sensitive process.
And like a small bug or a small error in the product can lead to incorrect payment.
Right.
And so, yeah, so we just kind of we still employed the team for QA for a bit to kind of figure out edge cases.
But at that point, once we had kind of turned it over to the QA and everything had been built out, mostly in the platform, that's when I kind of handed it off.
I still continue to manage the operations team, but we had the operations team themselves kind of own the process.
And so that's when I was able to kind of move on to the next thing, which was customer success, which was also very closely involved in this.
So, like, honestly, a lot of what I did at Everlane still revolved around this process.
And what I loved about my role is like at least 50 percent of my time was spent, like, working with products.
Whether it was on this or like, what's next?
Like, what do we need to build out next?
And so, yeah, I think like that was kind of the big milestone was we got our accuracy rate to zero or our error rate to zero.
mean, our delay rate to zero.
And then we were able to get it in a way that it was the product was producing what we needed.
We just had to check it and send it to admins.
And so that was when I was able to kind of move on.
That's awesome.
Um, and, um, knowing that, um, I know, I think we only scheduled 30 minutes.
you have a hard stop?
No, I don't.
Okay, cool.
Um, then we can keep, keep going for a little bit if you're okay.
Um, so you're throughout your whole tenure was your manager, Dan, the whole time?
No.
So actually after about a year, Dan left.
And so he had kind of recommended me to take over the rest of his responsibilities.
So I, uh, that's when I took over the customer success and the customer support team.
And I reported to the CEO after that.
Oh, nice.
Um, okay.
So, so in the time you were working for Dan, like how.
Yeah, think Dan would describe me as someone who really drives things across the finish line.
think that was something he really appreciated.
We worked really well together.
We would kind of like work together to brainstorm, which I love like talking through things.
And so that was really helpful for me.
And then, yeah, I would just kind of take it and run with it.
think he felt like I was very kind of proactive to just like identifying areas that need attention and just going after them, figuring out a plan and also just like being scrappy, I think.
And just like if we don't have the tools to build it, we were kind of a small startup.
You don't always have the budget to add a tool.
So just figuring out a way to make it work.
Like we made like a customer success task tracker using like Slack, Google Sheets, Google Sheets scripts, and like Zapier and like a free trial of Zapier.
And so I think those are ways you would describe me.
And what would you appreciate most about Dan as a leader and manager?

I my favorite part was just he, I've had a lot of managers who've made me feel like I need to prove myself in a job, but he from the beginning, and I don't know why, he just like assumed I could do everything he asked me to do and assumed I would do a good job.

And I feel like in a lot of ways it became a self-fulfilling prophecy because like, especially like given that I was a little bit earlier in my career than I am now, I was like, you think I could do this?

Like, I don't know if I can do this, you know, but just the fact that he knew, yeah, I was so confident that I could do it, that really like, honestly, that worked really well for us because it was never like a question of proving myself.
It was always like, I knew he had my back.
I knew he was there to support me.
And he was very, yeah, like very, like much my cheerleader throughout my time there.
And I think that really helped me like blossom and helped me grow into the responsibility and scope that I had.
Nice.
And then like, what's something that you wish you did differently?
I think honestly, think one thing that I wanted more of was just like feedback.
I think like still, since I was still early in my career, I'm still learning.
And I think like he was much more of like, oh, hey, like formal feedback.
Like he was not like super into formal feedback and like, you know, there's a time and place for it and there's not also.
And so like a lot of what we were doing was like flying by the seat of our pants.
So he was like, good job, like keep going, you know?
And so I think like versus coming from like such a structured world of feedback with consulting and then both at Mixpanel and Airtable, I worked with ex-consultants.
I was given a lot of like structured feedback.

It was a little different, but I think, yeah, that would be the one thing that I would want more of is just like what are areas that I can do better, especially as I was stepping into a management role and I hadn't done that before.
I think that would have been helpful.
Yeah, yeah.
And then are you currently managing folks as well now?
Yeah, I am.
Yeah, in the kind of like integration, we're kind of rejiggering the team.
So I'm managing less.
So I had a team of 14 prior to the integration, yeah.
Nice.
then, yeah, how prior to the integration, Ray, like how would your team describe you?
Yeah, I think they would describe me as kind of, hopefully they would describe me as a very trusting manager.
I really tried to give everyone a very long leash.
And I think like as long as I felt like they could execute on what we had kind of discussed, I think I really let them have a lot of autonomy.

And I think that was great for them and for me because it freed me up to do like special projects and things like that as well, while still kind of making sure that we were hitting our metrics, that people were happy.
And I think they would say that I was hopefully like a very honest boss.
I also learned that from Dan and that's something that I try to be is just like very candid about the situation of things and like not overly sugarcoating or overly like doomsdaying.
Just being like, hey, this is the situation.
And this is what we're going to do about it.
And we're going to, like, get to the next step.
And then we'll reevaluate what we want to do next.
Nice, nice.
And then, like, how would your peers across the organization or folks that you kind of interacted across, right?
Like, one, how would they describe you?
And then what constructive feedback do you think they would have for you?
Yeah, I think they would definitely say, like, I am very passionate and tenacious about things.
I think, like, especially with the product team, we've gotten into many kind of, like, battle, like, war rooms about, like, what do we build, when do we build it?
And so I think I'm just, like, very, my, I mean, given that I was kind of the post-salesperson, I was very passionate about making sure we build what our customers need.
And it was so easy to get caught up in the new exciting thing to build without fully building what you need for the infrastructure because people are supporting it today.
So it's like, okay, well, it's fine, right?
And so making, like, really, like...
I think passionate and also advocates for my team a lot.
I think people really saw that.

I would say that they probably, their feedback to me, and this is feedback that Dan actually gave me as well, was just making sure to really invest in the small talk, invest in the small things, I think in terms of building relationships with people.
Because like, for me, I've always kind of leaned on building relationships through working together.

But I think sometimes I tend to like, even in like meetings, if I'm hosting a meeting, just really jump in and be like, okay, so let's get down to business, you know, and just making sure you know that you build that space for small talk, because it does build, help build that relationship and help build kind of, yeah, just like the trust that you need to work together.
And so I think that was really good advice that I've been working on.
Nice.
And how has it been reporting directly to the CEO, in this case?
Yeah, it's been good.
I think I, what I love about it is I get a lot more autonomy, like even more so.

I think the drawback is I think that I don't get that like brainstorming partner as much, which I do love having, but I think comes with a little bit more of a hands-on manager, though I do feel like now that I've been reporting to the CEO for like over a year and a half, almost two years, I've grown more accustomed to it.
And I've kind of found the ways to build in that structured time for brainstorming during one-on-ones and then also find like other resources.
And that has been super valuable, like brainstorming with my teams, brainstorming with cross-functional partners, and like making sure I build that in as well has actually been really valuable.
So I think that's been kind of the main difference and the main drawback that I found just because obviously CEOs are less available.
But yeah, I think I've grown into it and I do enjoy kind of the autonomy and the ability to kind of, yeah, just run things the way that I see fit.
Nice.
And maybe just a few more and then like.
Okay, so since you think, look ahead, right?
Like, what is, like, really important to you?
And what I mean by that is, like, more of, like, bigger picture what you want to achieve and why, right?
Personally, right?
Like, meaning, like, professionally, but, like, for you individually, you know?
Yeah.
I think what I really want to achieve is, like, getting the chance to help scale a company again, but, like, past where we scaled last time, because Everlane got acquired kind of early.
We were only at 15 million in error, and we were really building momentum.
But I want to, like, take it to 50 million, 100 million, and just, like, see those stages for myself and see the challenges that come up and, like, learn how to navigate those really well.

And so I think for me, it's just really about, like, seeing a company scale and then internally really just getting more at-bats and opportunities to solve challenges, to understand deeply how, like, SaaS businesses work and how, like, different types of businesses work, too, and just really understanding, like, how to position a company.
And what are kind of the key points?
And like, I think in the future, I would love to be a COO.

And so just getting those reps, I think is really important of learning the businesses inside and out, being that really strong operator and getting, and I think like eventually, like I would love to grow teams again.
I'm in this next role.
I'm not necessarily looking for it because to me, I think the most important thing is being at a fast growing company, being at a company that has a really interesting product.
And then over time as the company evolves, if opportunities come up, for leadership again, then like, they'll always kind of, I'll always be excited about those.
But so that's why I'm not necessarily looking for it in my next role, but that is something I would eventually like to achieve again.
Yeah.
feel like managing people is overrated.
